ere's the full content to copy and paste:

---
title: "d1: Scaling Reasoning in Diffusion LLMs via Reinforcement Learning"
subtitle: "Paper Reading Notes — Log-Probability Estimation & diffu-GRPO"
author: ""
date: "`r Sys.Date()`"
output:
  html_document:
    toc: true
    toc_float: true
    theme: flatly
    highlight: tango
---

---

## Setup

Example: 

| Symbol | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Prompt $q$ | `"Xiao Ming has 3 apples, ate 1, how many are left?"` |
| Generated completion $o$ | `"2 are left"` — tokenized as 3 tokens: `[still]`, `[2]`, `[left]` |
| Goal | Estimate $\log p(\text{"2"} \mid q)$, the model's probability for token `"2"` at position 2 |

---

## Step-by-Step: One-Step Log-Probability Estimation

### Step 1 — Randomly mask part of the prompt

Apply random masking to the prompt with probability $p_{\text{mask}} = 0.15$, producing $q'$:
Original q:  [Xiao Ming] [has]  [3] [apples] [,] [ate] [1] [,] [how many] [left] [?]
Masked   q': [Xiao Ming]  [M]   [3] [apples] [,] [ate] [M] [,] [how many] [left] [?]

Most tokens survive; a small fraction are replaced by `[M]`.

---

### Step 2 — Set the entire completion side to MASK

We do **not** feed the already-generated completion $o$ back in.
Instead, every completion position becomes `[M]`:
Input to model:
[ Xiao Ming  [M]  3  apples  ,  ate  [M]  ,  how many  left  ? ]  [ [M]   [M]   [M] ]
<--------------------- q' (partially masked prompt) ------------>  <--- full MASK --->
pos1  pos2  pos3

---

### Step 3 — Single forward pass

The model outputs a probability distribution at every `[M]` position in one shot:
Model output (completion positions only):
pos1:  p("still") = 0.82,  p("answer") = 0.05,  p("total") = 0.03, ...
pos2:  p("2")     = 0.91,  p("1")      = 0.04,  p("3")     = 0.03, ...  <- target
pos3:  p("left")  = 0.88,  p("only")   = 0.06, ...

---

### Step 4 — Extract the token probability from $o$

Look up the probability of the actually-generated token at each completion position:

$$\log \hat{p}(o_2 = \text{"2"} \mid q) \approx \log(0.91) = -0.094$$

---

## Full Two-Stage Estimation Diagram

### Stage 1 — Outer loop (done once per prompt)
+---------------------------------------------------------+
|  Input:   [ prompt q ] + [ MASK  MASK  ...  MASK ]     |
|  Process: Full diffusion denoising (128 steps)         |
|  Output:  completion o = "2 are left"                  |
+---------------------------------------------------------+

### Stage 2 — Inner loop (once per mu gradient update, different masking pattern each time)
+---------------------------------------------------------------------+
|  Input:                                                             |
|  [ Xiao Ming [M] 3 apples ate [M] ? ]  +  [ [M]   [M]   [M]  ]    |
|  <---------- q' (randomly masked prompt) --------->  <- full MASK->|
|                                                      pos1 pos2 pos3 |
|                                                                     |
|  One forward pass                                                   |
|         |                                                           |
|         v                                                           |
|  Model outputs probability distributions at all [M] positions      |
|         |                                                           |
|         v                                                           |
|  Extract completion-position probabilities:                         |
|    pos1:  p("still") = 0.81  ->  log(0.81) = -0.210                |
|    pos2:  p("2")     = 0.91  ->  log(0.91) = -0.094                |
|    pos3:  p("left")  = 0.88  ->  log(0.88) = -0.128                |
|         |                                                           |
|         v                                                           |
|  log p_hat(o|q) = -0.210 + (-0.094) + (-0.128) = -0.432            |
+---------------------------------------------------------------------+

---

## diffu-GRPO: Full Training Loop

### Outer loop (requires online generation — expensive)
+-------------------------------------------------------------------+
|  1. Sample prompt q from dataset D                                |
|                                                                   |
|  2. Run full diffusion with old policy, generate G = 8            |
|     completions:                                                  |
|       o1 = "answer is 2",  o2 = "answer is 5", ..., o8 = "1"     |
|                                                                   |
|  3. Compute reward for each completion:                           |
|       r1 = 2.5,  r2 = 0.5,  ...,  r8 = 0.5                       |
|                                                                   |
|  4. Compute group-normalized advantage:                           |
|       A1 = +1.0,  A2 = -1.0,  ...                                 |
|                                                                   |
|  +-- Inner loop  mu = 12  (forward passes only -- cheap) ------+  |
|  |  Iteration n:                                               |  |
|  |  a. Randomly mask q -> q'_n  (new pattern each iteration)  |  |
|  |  b. One forward pass for theta, theta_old, theta_ref:       |  |
|  |       Input: [ q'_n  +  ALL-MASK completion ]               |  |
|  |       Extract per-token log-probs for o1, ..., o8           |  |
|  |  c. Compute objective:                                      |  |
|  |       ratio = p_theta(token) / p_theta_old(token)           |  |
|  |       loss  = clipped(ratio) x advantage  -  beta * KL      |  |
|  |  d. Gradient descent -> update theta                        |  |
|  +-------------------------------------------------------------+  |
|                                                                   |
|  5. After inner loop:  theta_old <- theta -> next outer step      |
+-------------------------------------------------------------------+

---

## Key Insight: Why Random Prompt Masking Helps

Each inner-loop iteration sees a **different masked view** $q'_n$ of the same prompt.
This acts as **implicit data augmentation** and **regularization**, preventing
the policy from overfitting within a single batch.

This allows scaling $\mu$ from the typical value of 2 up to **12 or even 24**,
so the model extracts more learning signal per expensive online generation —
dramatically reducing wall-clock training time.

---

## Summary of Notation

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| $q$ | Original prompt |
| $q'$ | Randomly masked version of $q$ |
| $o$ | Completion generated by the old policy |
| $p_{\text{mask}}$ | Probability of masking each prompt token (default 0.15) |
| $\mu$ | Number of inner gradient updates per outer batch |
| $G$ | Number of completions sampled per prompt (default 8) |
| $A_i^k$ | Advantage for token $k$ of completion $i$ |
| $\hat{p}(o \mid q)$ | Mean-field approximation of sequence log-probability |

---

*Notes based on: Zhao et al. (2025), "d1: Scaling Reasoning in Diffusion Large Language
Models via Reinforcement Learning." arXiv:2504.12216.*
